blocks.content: Daisy went with him. She didn't ask if he wanted her to — she just booked her flight when he booked his. He looked at the itinerary confirmation on the counter and didn't say anything. He pressed his hand flat to the paper for a moment the way he sometimes touched things he wanted to hold onto.

Ruth's house was the same as it had always been. The same photographs on the walls, the same too-small kitchen, the same particular smell of the place — coffee and cedar and something indefinably hers. She met them at the door in jeans and a cardigan and looked thinner than the summer but held her head the same way she always had: level, unbothered, present.

They sat in her kitchen for three hours that first night. She talked about the treatment — what it involved, what it didn't, what the doctors had said about timeline. She was clear and specific and answered every question he asked without making him feel like he was taking anything from her.
Daisy did the dishes. It was something to do with her hands.
At some point, past ten, Ruth reached across the table and covered Kit's hand with hers.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: I need you to hear me. I have had a good life. I have had you. I have had your film, and your wedding, and that girl doing my dishes right now — I have had all of that. Whatever comes next, I am not afraid of it.

She patted his hand. Got up to check on something on the stove. And Kit sat at his mother's kitchen table and did something Daisy had only seen him do twice in the years she'd known him as a grown man.
He put his face in his hands.
She dried her hands on the dish towel. Crossed the kitchen. Put her hand on his back.
Ruth came back to the table and did the same on the other side. Mother and wife, on either side of him.
He was not alone in it. That was the thing. He was not alone in it.
